title: Tearing chest-to-back pain after violent vomiting in an anticoagulated man

chart.oneLiner: 61M with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, type 2 diabetes, CKD, and hypertension presents after a heavy alcohol binge with sudden, severe, tearing lower-chest/epigastric pain radiating to the back that began at the height of a bout of forceful retching and vomiting 3 hours ago; he is febrile, tachycardic, and short of breath.

opening: I was throwing up really hard after drinking, and all of a sudden it felt like something tore in my chest — it hurts down here and straight through to my back, and it's hard to breathe.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 61-year-old man, anxious and uncomfortable, sweaty, breathing fast and shallow because deep breaths hurt. Smells of alcohol.

HPI:
- Severe, constant lower-chest and upper-abdominal (epigastric) pain that came on SUDDENLY right at the peak of a bout of violent, forceful vomiting/retching about 3 hours ago, at the end of a heavy drinking session and a large meal.
- Pain is sharp and TEARING, radiates straight through to the back and up to the left shoulder; worse with swallowing and with deep breaths (only if asked).
- Vomited several times; one of the last episodes had a little blood-streaked material (only if asked about vomiting/blood). He worries about the blood because he is 'on a blood thinner.'
- Feels short of breath and feverish/sweaty. Hasn't been able to keep anything down since.
- NO exertional onset, NO crushing/pressure quality typical of cardiac pain, NO migrating pain that started between the shoulder blades before the vomiting, NO leg swelling or calf pain, NO recent travel/immobility, NO known ulcer disease but does get heartburn.

OLDCARTS: abrupt onset 3h ago at the height of forceful vomiting; lower retrosternal/epigastric radiating to back and left shoulder; constant and severe; tearing/sharp; worse with swallowing and deep inspiration; severity 9/10; nothing relieves it.

PMH: atrial fibrillation (on a blood thinner), type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease ('my kidneys aren't great'), GERD/heartburn, hypertension. MEDICATIONS (reveal the list only if asked what he takes): apixaban (a blood thinner for his heart rhythm — took his morning dose today), metoprolol, amlodipine, metformin, atorvastatin, omeprazole intermittently. ALLERGIES: none. FAMILY: father had heart disease; otherwise noncontributory. SOCIAL: drinks heavily, especially on weekends — about a 12-pack of beer plus liquor on a binge day (only if asked about alcohol); current smoker ~30 pack-years (only if asked); no drug use.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY the system/maneuver requested; give normals when asked):
- General: ill-appearing, diaphoretic, tachypneic, splinting with breaths, anxious.
- Vitals: BP 102/64 (equal in both arms), HR 126 and IRREGULARLY irregular, RR 26, Temp 38.2°C, SpO2 92% RA.
- Skin/Chest wall: subcutaneous emphysema — crepitus (crackling) palpable over the lower anterior chest and base of the neck if palpated.
- Lungs: decreased breath sounds at the LEFT base with dullness (left pleural effusion); a crunching sound synchronous with the heartbeat over the precordium if carefully auscultated (Hamman crunch / mediastinal crepitus).
- Cardiac: tachycardic and irregularly irregular, no murmur; pulses symmetric and equal in both arms with NO inter-arm BP differential and NO pulse deficit.
- Abdomen: epigastric tenderness with mild voluntary guarding; not rigid/peritonitic; bowel sounds present.
- Neuro: intact; no focal deficit.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the heavy alcohol binge, the smoking, the blood-streaked vomit, the pain-with-swallowing, the medication names, or that the pain started exactly at the peak of forceful vomiting unless specifically asked.
- Subcutaneous emphysema/crepitus, the decreased left-base breath sounds, and the Hamman crunch are revealed only if the relevant maneuver is performed.
- Pulses and BP are equal in both arms with no pulse deficit (a pertinent negative against dissection) — reveal only if checked. The pulse is irregularly irregular (his known atrial fibrillation) — reveal only if checked.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.
- Use lay language; avoid medical jargon and textbook phrasing.

labs.CBC: WBC 18.6 (neutrophil-predominant leukocytosis with bands), Hgb 13.1, Plt 224 — leukocytosis consistent with mediastinitis/early sepsis; Hgb near baseline, arguing against major hemorrhage despite the anticoagulant.
labs.BMP: Na 133, K 4.6, Cl 96, HCO3 17, BUN 38, Cr 2.1 (baseline ~1.4 — acute kidney injury on CKD), glucose 188. Anion gap = 133 − (96 + 17) = 20 (elevated) — a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis driven by lactate and hypoperfusion.
labs.Lactate: 4.2 mmol/L (elevated) — accounts for most of the elevated anion gap and signals hypoperfusion/early sepsis from a contained perforation.
labs.Coags: INR 1.9 and anti-Xa elevated on apixaban (last dose this morning) compounded by the AKI; relevant to bleeding risk, the blood-streaked vomit, and the need for reversal before any operation.
labs.Troponin: Mildly elevated at 0.06 ng/mL (ref <0.04) but FLAT on repeat with no ischemic EKG changes — a type-2 (demand) pattern from tachycardia/sepsis, NOT a primary ACS; does not explain the pneumomediastinum.
labs.Lipase: Mildly elevated but not pancreatitis-range; not explanatory for the pneumomediastinum.
labs.Pleural_fluid: Left thoracentesis fluid: LOW pH (<6.0), markedly HIGH amylase, and undigested food particles — diagnostic of esophageal contents in the pleural space.
labs.CT_esophagram: CT chest with water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram: contrast extravasation from the distal LEFT posterolateral esophagus with pneumomediastinum and a left pleural effusion; the thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with NO intimal flap (excludes dissection) — confirms esophageal perforation (Boerhaave).

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~126 bpm): irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves and variable R-R intervals; NO ST-segment elevation and no reciprocal changes to suggest acute STEMI.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and no ST elevation. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.

ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and no ST elevation
searchTerms: atrial fibrillation rapid ventricular response ECG
atrial fibrillation no ST elevation 12 lead
ekg.clinicalVignette: A 61-year-old man with known atrial fibrillation presents with severe chest and epigastric pain after forceful vomiting following a large meal.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Pneumomediastinum with a left pleural effusion and subcutaneous/mediastinal air; left hydropneumothorax may be present (V sign of Naclerio). Mediastinum is NOT widened — no convincing sign of aortic dissection.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC chest film matching: Pneumomediastinum with left pleural effusion and mediastinal/subcutaneous air, without mediastinal widening.
cxr.imageDescription: Chest radiograph showing pneumomediastinum and left pleural effusion
cxr.clinicalVignette: A 61-year-old man presents with excruciating chest pain and neck swelling after repeated retching.

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis). Because this is a tearing chest-to-back pain that began with forceful vomiting in an anticoagulated patient, explicitly name the must-not-miss diagnoses you are working to exclude, AND name three distinct mechanisms/etiologies by which the esophagus can perforate.
steps.rubric: Esophageal rupture / Boerhaave syndrome (leading & CANT-MISS,8; accepts esophageal perforation). Aortic dissection — the key competing tearing chest-to-back diagnosis to exclude (CANT-MISS,4). Acute coronary syndrome / MI (CANT-MISS,3). Mallory-Weiss tear — the tempting post-emetic alternative in an anticoagulated patient, distinguished by the ABSENCE of pneumomediastinum/transmural air (core,2). Perforated peptic ulcer / pulmonary embolism / tension pneumothorax (core,2). Names the link between forceful vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema (Mackler triad) pointing to esophageal rupture (core,2). Names THREE mechanisms of esophageal perforation — e.g. barogenic/spontaneous (Boerhaave from forceful vomiting), iatrogenic/instrumentation (endoscopy, dilation), and foreign body / caustic ingestion / penetrating trauma / malignancy (core,4). PENALTY: anchoring on a benign cause (GERD/musculoskeletal) or on a Mallory-Weiss bleed and not naming a transmural/vascular life-threat (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome). The competing tearing chest-to-back diagnosis that MUST be excluded is aortic dissection; also exclude acute coronary syndrome / MI. A Mallory-Weiss tear is the tempting trap here (post-vomiting, blood-streaked emesis, on an anticoagulant) but it is mucosal and does NOT cause pneumomediastinum or subcutaneous emphysema. Also reasonable: perforated peptic ulcer, pulmonary embolism, pneumothorax. Three mechanisms of esophageal perforation: (1) barogenic/spontaneous — Boerhaave, a sudden rise in intraesophageal pressure against a closed glottis during forceful vomiting; (2) iatrogenic — endoscopy, dilation, NG/TEE instrumentation (the most common cause overall); (3) foreign body or caustic ingestion, penetrating/blunt trauma, or an eroding malignancy.

criticalActions.item: Aortic dissection — competing tearing chest-to-back diagnosis to exclude

coreActions.item: Mallory-Weiss tear (mucosal post-emetic bleed — distinguished by no pneumomediastinum)
Perforated peptic ulcer / pulmonary embolism / pneumothorax
Names the link between forceful vomiting, chest pain, and subcutaneous emphysema (Mackler triad) pointing to esophageal rupture
Names three mechanisms of esophageal perforation (barogenic/Boerhaave; iatrogenic/instrumentation; foreign body/caustic/trauma/malignancy)

penalties.item: anchoring on a benign cause (GERD/musculoskeletal) or a Mallory-Weiss bleed without naming a transmural/vascular life-threat

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What workup do you want? (And what must you check at the bedside?)
steps.rubric: CXR looking for pneumomediastinum/pleural effusion (CANT-MISS,4). CT chest with water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram — confirmatory and simultaneously images the aorta to exclude dissection (CANT-MISS,5). EKG and troponin to exclude ACS (core,1). CBC/BMP/lactate to gauge sepsis and AKI (core,1). Coagulation panel / anti-Xa given apixaban use (core,1). Pleural fluid analysis (pH, amylase) if effusion tapped (core,1). Lipase (core,1). Type and screen given anticoagulation and likely surgery (core,1). PENALTY: ordering a BARIUM swallow (mediastinal barium is harmful in suspected perforation) (-3); giving the patient anything by mouth / oral barium (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; CBC; BMP; lactate; coagulation panel / anti-Xa (he is on apixaban); type and screen; Troponin; Lipase; pleural fluid analysis if tapped; and a CT chest with WATER-SOLUBLE (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram — which both confirms the perforation and images the aorta to exclude the competing dissection. Avoid barium and keep him strictly NPO.

criticalActions.item: CXR looking for pneumomediastinum / pleural effusion
CT chest with water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram (also images the aorta to exclude dissection)

coreActions.item: EKG and troponin to exclude ACS
Type and screen in anticipation of surgery

penalties.item: ordering a barium swallow (mediastinal barium is harmful in suspected perforation)
giving the patient oral barium / anything by mouth

requiredTests: CXR looking for pneumomediastinum / pleural effusion
CT chest with water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast esophagram (also images the aorta to exclude dissection)
optionalTests: EKG and troponin to exclude ACS
Type and screen in anticipation of surgery
contraindicatedTests: ordering a barium swallow (mediastinal barium is harmful in suspected perforation)
orderSensitiveActions: ordering a barium swallow (mediastinal barium is harmful in suspected perforation)

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings and how they fit the clinical picture.
steps.rubric: Findings = atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm — recognizes the irregularly irregular rhythm with absent P waves as atrial fibrillation with RVR (matches his known AF and his anticoagulation) (7); EXPLICITLY recognizes there is NO ST elevation / no STEMI so ACS is not the primary driver and the rapid rate reflects pain/sepsis (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia and diverting to a cath lab / thrombolytics, which would be catastrophic in an anticoagulated patient with a perforation (-3).
keyFindings: identifies atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
recognizes no ST elevation — not a STEMI
criticalFindings: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and no ST-segment elevation (not an acute STEMI).
commonMistakes: over-reading nonspecific changes as acute ischemia and anchoring on ACS
mistaking the irregular rhythm for a primary arrhythmic emergency rather than rate-related demand from sepsis/pain
steps.idealAnswer: Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular response (~126 bpm): irregularly irregular, absent P waves, variable R-R intervals — concordant with his known atrial fibrillation (hence the apixaban). There is NO ST-segment elevation; this is not an acute STEMI, and the rapid rate is driven by pain, fever, and early sepsis from the perforation.

criticalActions: Atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and no ST-segment elevation (not an acute STEMI).
coreActions: identifies atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
recognizes no ST elevation — not a STEMI

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = PNEUMOMEDIASTINUM with a left pleural effusion and mediastinal/subcutaneous air, WITHOUT mediastinal widening. CREDIT: identifies the pneumomediastinum / left effusion (6); links it to a suspected esophageal perforation and notes the non-widened mediastinum argues against dissection (4). PENALTY: calling the film normal / missing the free air (-3).
keyFindings: identifies the pneumomediastinum and left pleural effusion
links it to suspected esophageal perforation
criticalFindings: Pneumomediastinum with left pleural effusion and mediastinal/subcutaneous air; no mediastinal widening.
commonMistakes: calling the film normal or attributing the effusion to simple pneumonia and missing the free air
steps.idealAnswer: Pneumomediastinum with a left pleural effusion and mediastinal/subcutaneous air — concerning for esophageal perforation. The mediastinum is not widened, which argues against (though does not fully exclude) aortic dissection.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Pneumomediastinum with left pleural effusion and mediastinal/subcutaneous air; no mediastinal widening.

coreActions.item: identifies the pneumomediastinum and left pleural effusion
links it to suspected esophageal perforation

penalties.item: calling the film normal / missing the free air

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the pneumomediastinum, left effusion, the pleural-fluid result, and a CT esophagram showing contrast extravasation from the distal esophagus with a normal aorta, commit to your diagnosis. Explain the single discriminating test, what excludes the competing diagnoses (dissection, ACS, Mallory-Weiss), and why this matters now.
steps.rubric: Esophageal rupture / Boerhaave syndrome (6; accepts spontaneous esophageal perforation). Names the CT water-soluble esophagram as the discriminating/confirmatory test; ties the pleural fluid (low pH, high amylase, food particles) and the Mackler triad to the diagnosis; explains the normal aorta on CT excludes dissection, the flat troponin/AF-RVR EKG excludes primary ACS, and the transmural air excludes a mucosal-only Mallory-Weiss tear; recognizes it is a surgical emergency whose mortality climbs sharply with delayed treatment (mediastinitis/sepsis) — compounded here by his anticoagulation and AKI (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome). The discriminating test is the CT chest with water-soluble (Gastrografin) esophagram showing contrast extravasation from the distal esophagus; the pleural fluid with pH <6.0, high amylase, and food particles is confirmatory, completing the Mackler triad. The competing diagnoses are excluded: the CT aorta is normal with no intimal flap (not dissection), the troponin is flat with AF-RVR and no ST elevation (not primary ACS), and the transmural air/pneumomediastinum rules out a mucosa-only Mallory-Weiss tear. This is a time-critical surgical emergency — mortality rises steeply after 24 hours — and his apixaban plus AKI raise the bleeding and operative risk that must be managed urgently.

steps.label: Management
steps.prompt: What is your management plan, including disposition? Address why this patient is NOT safe for discharge or observation, and how his anticoagulation and kidney injury change your plan.
steps.rubric: Per MGH approach to esophageal perforation: keep NPO and decompress carefully (CANT-MISS,3); broad-spectrum IV antibiotics PLUS antifungal coverage for mediastinitis (CANT-MISS,4); STAT thoracic/general surgery consult — early operative repair (<24h) markedly improves survival (CANT-MISS,5); REVERSE the apixaban (e.g., andexanet alfa or PCC) before operative repair and hold further anticoagulation — a CANT-MISS step given the bleeding risk (CANT-MISS,3); aggressive IV fluid resuscitation +/- vasopressors for sepsis, with renal-protective/contrast-aware dosing given AKI (3); rate-control his AF rapid ventricular response while resuscitating (2); pleural/mediastinal drainage (chest tube) (2); IV PPI and pain control (1); admit to ICU and EXPLICITLY state this is not safe for discharge/observation — a contained perforation can decompensate into fulminant mediastinitis/sepsis (2). PENALTY: oral intake or BARIUM contrast (-3); discharging or observing / treating as benign GERD or Mallory-Weiss (-4); thrombolysis or cath-lab activation for a misread STEMI (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Treat as a surgical emergency and admit to the ICU — this patient is NOT safe for discharge or observation because a contained esophageal perforation can rapidly progress to fulminant mediastinitis and septic shock. Keep strictly NPO; resuscitate with IV fluids (pressors if septic), using renal-aware, contrast-sparing care given his AKI on CKD. Start broad-spectrum IV antibiotics WITH antifungal coverage for mediastinitis. STAT thoracic/general surgery consult for early operative repair — primary repair within 24 hours dramatically improves survival; endoscopic stenting/drainage if not an operative candidate. Crucially, REVERSE his apixaban (andexanet alfa or 4-factor PCC) and hold anticoagulation before any operation. Rate-control the atrial-fibrillation rapid ventricular response as you resuscitate. Add IV PPI, pain control, and chest-tube/mediastinal drainage of the contaminated pleural space. Avoid all oral intake and any barium contrast. Do NOT thrombolyse or activate the cath lab — the EKG is AF with RVR, not a STEMI.

criticalActions.item: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics plus antifungal coverage
STAT thoracic / general surgery consult for early operative repair
Reverse apixaban (andexanet alfa / PCC) and hold anticoagulation before surgery

coreActions.item: Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation (pressors if septic), renal-aware given AKI
Rate-control the atrial-fibrillation rapid ventricular response
Admit to ICU; explicitly NOT safe for discharge or observation

penalties.item: Discharging / observing / treating as benign GERD or Mallory-Weiss
Thrombolysis or cath-lab activation for a misread STEMI

physicalExamMappings.finding: Skin/Chest wall: subcutaneous emphysema — crepitus (crackling) palpable over the lower anterior chest and base of the neck.
Lungs: decreased breath sounds at the LEFT base with dullness (left pleural effusion); a crunching sound synchronous with the heartbeat over the precordium (Hamman crunch / mediastinal crepitus).
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Breath sounds clear and equal bilaterally; no added sounds.

physicalExamMappings.finding: General: ill-appearing, diaphoretic, tachypneic, splinting with breaths, anxious.
physicalExamMappings.normalIfNotPresent: Patient appears comfortable and in no distress.

physicalExamMappings.finding: Abdomen: epigastric tenderness with mild voluntary guarding; not rigid/peritonitic; bowel sounds present.

Cardiac/pulses: tachycardic and IRREGULARLY irregular (known atrial fibrillation); pulses symmetric and equal in both arms with no inter-arm BP differential and no pulse deficit (pertinent negatives against aortic dissection).

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 61M with atrial fibrillation on apixaban, type 2 diabetes, CKD, and hypertension presents after a heavy alcohol binge with sudden, severe, tearing lower-chest/epigastric pain radiating to the back that began at the height of forceful retching and vomiting 3 hours ago. The diagnosis is Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome), reached by eliciting the focused history (forceful vomiting immediately preceding the pain), performing the targeted exam (subcutaneous crepitus, Hamman crunch, left effusion, equal pulses against dissection), and interpreting a directed, order-sensitive workup (CXR pneumomediastinum, CT water-soluble esophagram that simultaneously excludes dissection). The picture is layered by a high-anion-gap metabolic acidosis with elevated lactate, AKI on CKD, and supratherapeutic anticoagulation that change resuscitation and pre-operative management, while atrial fibrillation with RVR on the EKG is a deliberate not-a-STEMI distractor.
keyHistoryClues: forceful vomiting immediately before the pain
tearing chest-to-back pain that did NOT start in the back
atrial fibrillation on apixaban (a blood thinner)
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Skin/Chest wall: subcutaneous emphysema — crepitus palpable over the lower anterior chest and base of the neck.
Lungs: decreased breath sounds at the LEFT base with dullness; Hamman crunch over the precordium.
General: ill-appearing, diaphoretic, tachypneic, splinting with breaths, anxious.
Abdomen: epigastric tenderness with mild voluntary guarding; not rigid.
Cardiac/pulses: irregularly irregular pulse; equal in both arms with no pulse deficit (against dissection).
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals, noting the irregularly irregular rapid pulse and hypoxia.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning — especially that forceful vomiting preceded the pain and that he takes a blood thinner for atrial fibrillation.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal subcutaneous crepitus, a left effusion, a Hamman crunch, and equal pulses arguing against dissection.

Build a differential led by Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome) while explicitly excluding aortic dissection and ACS and recognizing Mallory-Weiss as a trap; name three mechanisms of esophageal perforation.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup (CXR, CT water-soluble esophagram that also images the aorta, coagulation panel) and interpret the AF-with-RVR EKG as not-a-STEMI; avoid barium.

Commit to Esophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome), integrate the acidosis/lactate/AKI/anticoagulation, and manage with NPO, fluids, broad antibiotics + antifungal, anticoagulant reversal, drainage, an emergent surgical consult, and ICU admission — explicitly not safe for discharge.
commonPitfalls: Discharging / observing / treating as benign GERD or a Mallory-Weiss bleed
Misreading AF with RVR as a STEMI and thrombolysing an anticoagulated patient with a perforation
Forgetting to reverse apixaban before operative repair
managementPearls: Broad-spectrum IV antibiotics plus antifungal coverage
STAT thoracic / general surgery consult for early operative repair
Reverse apixaban (andexanet alfa / PCC) before surgery

imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental or chronic abnormality (here, the patient's atrial fibrillation) is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, explain the rapid rate, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives — no ST elevation, no mediastinal widening.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
